    Antonov An-225 Mriya

    Antonov · Cargo · Destroyed (2022) — one built

    The largest aircraft ever built.

    Length84 m
    Wingspan88.4 m
    Range15,400 km
    MTOW640 t
    Built1
    First flight1988
    Our numbers

    Derived metrics

    Computed by aircraft.fyi from the core specification — see how we calculate these.

    Range per tonne#35 of 40
    24.1 km/t maximum range ÷ MTOW in tonnes
    Span-to-length ratio#8 of 43
    1.05 wingspan ÷ fuselage length

    Last verified: 2026-07-14 · Spot an error? business@luck.fyi

    The story

    What makes it different

    Mriya means "dream", and the An-225 was built for exactly one: to carry the Soviet Buran space shuttle on its back. Antonov took the already-enormous An-124, stretched it, added two more engines for a total of six, doubled the landing gear to 32 wheels, and replaced the single fin with a twin tail so the shuttle's wake wouldn't hammer it. The result was the heaviest aircraft ever flown — 640 tonnes at maximum weight — and at 84 metres, the longest.

    Buran flew only once before the programme was shelved in the early 1990s, and the An-225 went on to a second career as the world's ultimate outsize freighter. When something was too big or too heavy for anything else — 190-tonne generators, wind turbine blades, relief cargo by the hundreds of tonnes — Mriya was the phone call. It held over 200 payload-related world records, and everywhere it landed, crowds came to watch.

    Only one An-225 was ever completed. It was destroyed in 2022 while undergoing maintenance. A second airframe, roughly one-third built since the 1990s, still exists, and Antonov has said the type may one day be completed. Its records remain unbeaten.

    Variants

    The Antonov An-225 Mriya line

    An-225 Mriya
    The sole completed aircraft, UR-82060 — destroyed in 2022.
    Second airframe
    Roughly one-third built in the 1990s and stored since. Antonov has said it may one day be completed.
    Operators

    Who flies the Antonov An-225 Mriya

    All 1 (historical) current operators, verified 2026-07-14. Fleet counts are approximate where marked ≈.

    UAAntonov AirlinesThe sole operator throughout the aircraft's life — Soviet state operation until 1991, then Antonov's own outsize-cargo airline until the aircraft's destruction in 2022.—

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
